### 1) Structural consequences of the condition

Given \(|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)\) for all \(i,j\), note that for \(g=\gcd(i,j)\) we have \(g\mid i\) and \(g\mid j\), so
\[
|A_g|=g,\qquad |A_i\cap A_j|=g.
\]
Also \(A_g\subseteq A_i\) and \(A_g\subseteq A_j\) because \(g\mid i\Rightarrow |A_g\cap A_i|=\gcd(g,i)=g=|A_g|\), so \(A_g\subseteq A_i\) (similarly for \(j\)). Hence
\[
A_g\subseteq A_i\cap A_j\quad\text{and}\quad |A_g|=|A_i\cap A_j|,
\]
so we actually get the stronger identity
\[
A_i\cap A_j = A_{\gcd(i,j)} \quad \text{(as sets)}.
\]

In particular, if \(d\mid n\) then \(A_d\subseteq A_n\).

---

### 2) Decomposition into disjoint “new-element blocks” \(B_n\)

Define, for each \(n\ge 1\),
\[
B_n \;=\; A_n \setminus \bigcup_{\substack{d\mid n\\ d<n}} A_d.
\]
These are the elements that appear “for the first time” at index \(n\). Because every proper divisor set \(A_d\) is contained in \(A_n\), this is well-defined.

**Claim:** The sets \(B_n\) are pairwise disjoint.

If \(x\in B_m\cap B_n\), then \(x\in A_m\cap A_n = A_{\gcd(m,n)}\). Let \(g=\gcd(m,n)\). Then \(x\in A_g\subseteq A_m\). But \(x\in B_m\) means \(x\notin A_d\) for any proper divisor \(d\mid m\). Since \(g\mid m\), this forces \(g=m\). Similarly \(g=n\), hence \(m=n\).

Thus the \(B_n\) are disjoint, and moreover
\[
A_n \;=\; \bigsqcup_{d\mid n} B_d
\]
(disjoint union).

Taking sizes and using \(|A_n|=n\) (from \(|A_n|=|A_n\cap A_n|=\gcd(n,n)=n\)):
\[
n = |A_n| = \sum_{d\mid n} |B_d|.
\]
By Möbius inversion, this forces
\[
|B_n| = \varphi(n)\quad\text{for all }n.
\]

So the entire structure is rigid up to relabeling of the underlying positive integers.

---

### 3) Restricting attention to divisors of \(250\)

We only need \(\max A_d\) for \(d\mid 250\), where
\[
250 = 2\cdot 5^3,\quad d\in\{1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250\}.
\]

Since \(A_{250}\) has size \(250\), any set \(A_{250}\) has \(\max A_{250}\ge 250\). To minimize the sum, we should label the elements in \(A_{250}\) using \(\{1,2,\dots,250\}\), making \(\max A_{250}=250\). All elements not in \(A_{250}\) (i.e. in blocks \(B_n\) with \(n\nmid 250\)) can be labeled with integers \(>250\), not affecting any \(\max A_d\) for \(d\mid 250\).

Thus we only need to assign the numbers \(1,2,\dots,250\) to the blocks \(B_d\) for \(d\mid 250\), with fixed block sizes \(|B_d|=\varphi(d)\):

\[
\begin{array}{c|cccccccc}
d & 1&2&5&10&25&50&125&250\\ \hline
\varphi(d)&1&1&4&4&20&20&100&100
\end{array}
\]
(sum \(=250\), as expected).

Also,
\[
A_d = \bigsqcup_{e\mid d} B_e.
\]

---

### 4) Optimal labeling becomes an ordering problem

Let \(m_e = \max B_e\). Then
\[
\max A_d = \max_{e\mid d} m_e.
\]

Key fact: If we sort the blocks by increasing \(m_e\), then for the \(k\)-th block in this sorted order with maximum \(M\), the total number of elements in the first \(k\) blocks is \(\sum \varphi(e)\), and all those elements are \(\le M\). Hence
\[
M \;\ge\; \text{(total size of those blocks)}.
\]
Therefore, for any chosen *order* of blocks, the minimal possible maxima occur when we assign consecutive integers to blocks in that order (first block gets the smallest \(\varphi\)-many integers, next block gets the next \(\varphi\)-many, etc.). So we may assume wlog that each block \(B_e\) receives a consecutive interval, and then \(m_e\) is just the cumulative total size up to that block.

So we just need the best order of the 8 blocks.

---

### 5) Determining the optimal order

Heuristically (and can be justified by swap arguments): large blocks that affect only few of the \(\max A_d\) should go late; blocks needed for small \(d\) should go early.

- \(B_{250}\) affects only \(\max A_{250}\), and \(\max A_{250}\) is forced to be \(250\) anyway, so \(B_{250}\) should be last.
- If \(B_{125}\) were placed before \(B_{50}\), then \(\max A_{50}\) (which does **not** need \(B_{125}\)) would be inflated by 100, while \(\max A_{125}\) could decrease by at most 20 from swapping; net worse. So \(B_{50}\) must come before \(B_{125}\). Similarly \(B_{125}\) should be just before \(B_{250}\).
- Within the first six blocks \(\{1,2,5,10,25,50\}\), putting \(B_{50}\) earlier only delays \(\max A_2,\max A_5,\max A_{10},\max A_{25}\) while \(\max A_{50}\) cannot drop below \(50\) (since those six blocks total size \(50\)). So \(B_{50}\) should be last among those six.
- To keep \(\max A_{10}\) small, we must place \(B_2,B_5,B_{10}\) before the big \(B_{25}\).
- Between \(B_2\) (size 1) and \(B_5\) (size 4): if \(B_5\) comes before \(B_2\), then \(\max A_2\) increases by 4 while \(\max A_5\) decreases by only 1; net worse. So \(B_2\) should precede \(B_5\).
- Between \(B_5\) and \(B_{10}\) (both size 4): swapping them does not change \(\max A_{10}\), but it does change \(\max A_5\), so we want \(B_5\) before \(B_{10}\).

This uniquely yields the optimal order:
\[
B_1,\; B_2,\; B_5,\; B_{10},\; B_{25},\; B_{50},\; B_{125},\; B_{250}.
\]

---

### 6) Compute all \(\max A_d\) and the sum

Assign consecutive labels:

- \(B_1:\{1\}\) so \(m_1=1\)
- \(B_2:\{2\}\) so \(m_2=2\)
- \(B_5:\{3,4,5,6\}\) so \(m_5=6\)
- \(B_{10}:\{7,8,9,10\}\) so \(m_{10}=10\)
- \(B_{25}:\{11,\dots,30\}\) so \(m_{25}=30\)
- \(B_{50}:\{31,\dots,50\}\) so \(m_{50}=50\)
- \(B_{125}:\{51,\dots,150\}\) so \(m_{125}=150\)
- \(B_{250}:\{151,\dots,250\}\) so \(m_{250}=250\)

Now for each divisor \(d\mid 250\), \(\max A_d=\max_{e\mid d} m_e\):

\[
\begin{array}{c|c}
d & \max A_d \\ \hline
1 & 1\\
2 & 2\\
5 & 6\\
10 & 10\\
25 & 30\\
50 & 50\\
125 & 150\\
250 & 250
\end{array}
\]

Sum:
\[
1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250 = 499.
\]

Therefore the minimum possible value is \(\boxed{499}\).

### 1) Immediate consequences of the condition
Given \(|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)\), setting \(i=j\) gives
\[
|A_i|=\gcd(i,i)=i.
\]
In particular, \(|A_{250}|=250\), so \(\max A_{250}\ge 250\). To minimize the required sum we must have \(\max A_{250}=250\), hence (after relabeling elements) we may assume
\[
A_{250}=\{1,2,\dots,250\}.
\]

If \(d\mid 250\), then \(\gcd(d,250)=d\), so
\[
|A_d\cap A_{250}|=d=|A_d|\implies A_d\subseteq A_{250}.
\]
Moreover, if \(d\mid e\) (both divisors of \(250\)), then \(\gcd(d,e)=d\) and similarly \(A_d\subseteq A_e\).

So the sets \(A_d\) for \(d\mid 250\) form an inclusion system ordered by divisibility, all living inside \(\{1,\dots,250\}\).

---

### 2) Forced “new element” block sizes (Möbius inversion \(\Rightarrow\) Euler \(\varphi\))
For each divisor \(n\mid 250\), define the “new elements appearing at level \(n\)” by
\[
E_n \;=\; A_n\setminus \bigcup_{\substack{d\mid n\\ d<n}} A_d.
\]
Because \(A_d\subseteq A_n\) whenever \(d\mid n\), these \(E_n\) are disjoint and
\[
A_n=\bigsqcup_{d\mid n} E_d
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
|A_n|=\sum_{d\mid n}|E_d|.
\]
Let \(e_n=|E_n|\). Since \(|A_n|=n\), we have
\[
n=\sum_{d\mid n} e_d.
\]
By Möbius inversion on divisors, the unique solution is \(e_n=\varphi(n)\). Thus the sizes of these disjoint blocks are **forced**:
\[
\begin{array}{c|cccccccc}
n & 1&2&5&10&25&50&125&250\\ \hline
\varphi(n) & 1&1&4&4&20&20&100&100
\end{array}
\]
and \(A_{250}\) is the disjoint union of these blocks.

Also, for each \(d\mid 250\),
\[
A_d=\bigsqcup_{n\mid d} E_n,
\]
so \(\max A_d\) depends only on the largest label used among the blocks \(E_n\) with \(n\mid d\).

---

### 3) Reducing to an ordering problem
We must assign the labels \(1,2,\dots,250\) to the disjoint blocks \(E_n\) (with the above sizes). Since gaps only increase maxima, we use exactly \(1,\dots,250\).

For minimizing maxima of unions, we may assume each block occupies a contiguous chunk of labels, and the problem becomes: **choose an order of the blocks**, place them consecutively, and compute the induced maxima.

We now determine the optimal order.

---

### 4) Determining the optimal order (exchange/impact arguments)

- \(E_{250}\) affects only \(A_{250}\), whose maximum is already forced to be \(250\). Putting \(E_{250}\) earlier would only push other blocks later and increase other maxima. So \(E_{250}\) should be **last**.

- \(E_{125}\) affects \(A_{125}\) and \(A_{250}\). If \(E_{125}\) were placed before any block needed for \(A_{50}\), it would delay the completion of \(A_{50}\) by \(100\), making \(\max A_{50}\ge 150\), while \(\max A_{125}\) can never go below \(125\); the net change is bad. Hence all blocks needed for \(A_{50}\), namely
  \[
  E_2,E_5,E_{10},E_{25},E_{50},
  \]
  must come **before** \(E_{125}\).

- Within \(\{E_2,E_5,E_{10},E_{25},E_{50}\}\), note \(E_{50}\) affects only \(A_{50}\) and \(A_{250}\). Putting \(E_{50}\) earlier only delays blocks that affect additional maxima (\(A_{25},A_{10},A_5,A_2\)) without reducing any of those. So \(E_{50}\) should be **last among these five**.

Now we just order \(E_2,E_5,E_{10},E_{25}\):

- Swapping \(E_{25}\) before \(E_{10}\) increases \(\max A_{10}\) by \(20\) (since \(E_{25}\not\mid 10\), it delays \(E_{10}\)), but can decrease \(\max A_{25}\) by at most \(4\). Net worse \(\Rightarrow E_{10}\) before \(E_{25}\).

- Swapping \(E_5\) after \(E_{10}\) delays \(A_5\) by \(4\) while leaving \(\max A_{10}\) unchanged (it’s still determined by the later of those two size-4 blocks). So \(E_5\) should be before \(E_{10}\).

- Finally, swapping \(E_2\) after \(E_5\) increases \(\max A_2\) by \(4\) and does not improve any other relevant maximum. So \(E_2\) should be before \(E_5\).

Therefore the optimal block order is
\[
E_1,\ E_2,\ E_5,\ E_{10},\ E_{25},\ E_{50},\ E_{125},\ E_{250}.
\]

---

### 5) Compute the resulting maxima and the sum
Place these blocks consecutively using labels \(1\) through \(250\). Their ending positions (hence their maximum labels) are:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\max E_1=1,\\
&\max E_2=2,\\
&\max E_5=2+4=6,\\
&\max E_{10}=6+4=10,\\
&\max E_{25}=10+20=30,\\
&\max E_{50}=30+20=50,\\
&\max E_{125}=50+100=150,\\
&\max E_{250}=150+100=250.
\end{aligned}
\]
Now use \(A_d=\bigsqcup_{n\mid d}E_n\), so \(\max A_d\) is the largest endpoint among \(E_n\) with \(n\mid d\):
\[
\begin{array}{c|cccccccc}
d & 1&2&5&10&25&50&125&250\\ \hline
\max A_d & 1&2&6&10&30&50&150&250
\end{array}
\]
Sum:
\[
1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250=499.
\]

A full infinite sequence \((A_i)\) satisfying the condition for all \(i,j\) exists via the standard construction using disjoint blocks of size \(\varphi(n)\) for every \(n\), and we have optimized the labels on the blocks that divide \(250\).

\[
\boxed{499}
\]

### 1) Immediate consequences of \(|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)\)

Taking \(i=j\) gives
\[
|A_i|=|A_i\cap A_i|=\gcd(i,i)=i.
\]

If \(i\mid j\), then \(\gcd(i,j)=i\), so
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=i=|A_i|\implies A_i\subseteq A_j.
\]

For general \(i,j\), let \(g=\gcd(i,j)\). Since \(g\mid i\) and \(g\mid j\), we have \(A_g\subseteq A_i\) and \(A_g\subseteq A_j\), hence \(A_g\subseteq A_i\cap A_j\). But
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=g=|A_g|,
\]
so in fact
\[
A_i\cap A_j = A_{\gcd(i,j)}.
\]

So the intersections are exactly the gcd-sets, not just in size.

---

### 2) The forced “new elements” decomposition and Euler \(\varphi\)

Define the “new part” of \(A_n\) by
\[
B_n \;=\; A_n\setminus \bigcup_{\substack{d\mid n\\ d<n}} A_d.
\]
Then \(A_n\) is a disjoint union
\[
A_n=\bigsqcup_{d\mid n} B_d,
\]
because if an element lay in both \(B_a\) and \(B_b\), it would lie in \(A_a\cap A_b=A_{\gcd(a,b)}\), forcing it into a smaller divisor set, contradicting “newness.”

Taking sizes:
\[
n=|A_n|=\sum_{d\mid n} |B_d|.
\]
By Möbius inversion, the unique solution is
\[
|B_n|=\varphi(n).
\]

So, up to relabeling of the underlying integers, the structure of the sets is forced: disjoint blocks \(B_n\) of size \(\varphi(n)\), and \(A_n\) is the union of blocks over divisors of \(n\).

---

### 3) Specialize to divisors of \(250\)

The positive divisors of \(250=2\cdot 5^3\) are
\[
1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250.
\]
Compute \(\varphi\) on these:
\[
\varphi(1)=1,\ \varphi(2)=1,\ \varphi(5)=4,\ \varphi(10)=4,\ \varphi(25)=20,\ \varphi(50)=20,\ \varphi(125)=100,\ \varphi(250)=100.
\]
These sum to \(250\), so \(A_{250}\) consists exactly of these \(250\) elements.

To minimize \(\max A_{250}\), we must take \(A_{250}=\{1,2,\dots,250\}\), hence
\[
\max A_{250}=250.
\]
Thus we are partitioning \(\{1,\dots,250\}\) into 8 disjoint blocks of the above sizes.

Also, for each divisor \(d\mid 250\),
\[
A_d=\bigcup_{t\mid d} B_t,
\]
so \(\max A_d\) is the maximum label appearing in the union of those divisor-blocks.

---

### 4) Reduce the minimization to an ordering (scheduling) problem

Let \(m_t=\max B_t\). Then
\[
\max A_d=\max_{t\mid d} m_t.
\]

If \(t\mid d\) but \(m_t>m_d\), then \(B_t\subseteq A_d\), so \(\max A_d\ge m_t\). Swapping the largest elements of \(B_t\) and \(B_d\) decreases \(m_t\) and increases \(m_d\), but **does not increase any \(\max A_x\)**, because every set that contains \(B_d\) also contains \(B_t\). Repeating, we may assume wlog that
\[
t\mid d \implies m_t\le m_d,
\]
so \(\max A_d = m_d\) for each \(d\mid 250\).

Now we only need minimize
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250} m_d
\]
subject to disjoint blocks of fixed sizes. If we list the blocks in increasing order of their maxima, we can “compress” so that the blocks occupy consecutive intervals starting at 1 (this never increases any maximum). Hence the maximum of each block becomes the cumulative endpoint.

So we are choosing a **topological order** respecting divisibility, and each block’s maximum is its **completion time** in that order, with processing times \(\varphi(d)\). Minimizing \(\sum m_d\) becomes minimizing the sum of completion times.

Precedence constraints (divisibility) among the 8 blocks are:
\[
1\to 2\to 10\to 50\to 250,\qquad 1\to 5\to 25\to 125\to 250,\qquad 5\to 10,\qquad 25\to 50.
\]

Whenever two available blocks are incomparable, putting the shorter one first decreases the sum of completion times (standard adjacent-swap argument). Apply this at each choice point:

- After \(1\): available \(2(1)\), \(5(4)\) \(\Rightarrow\) do \(2\) then \(5\).
- Then available \(10(4)\), \(25(20)\) \(\Rightarrow\) do \(10\) then \(25\).
- Then available \(50(20)\), \(125(100)\) \(\Rightarrow\) do \(50\) then \(125\).
- Finally \(250(100)\).

Thus the optimal block order is
\[
1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250.
\]

Compute cumulative endpoints (= maxima):
\[
\begin{aligned}
m_1&=1\\
m_2&=1+1=2\\
m_5&=2+4=6\\
m_{10}&=6+4=10\\
m_{25}&=10+20=30\\
m_{50}&=30+20=50\\
m_{125}&=50+100=150\\
m_{250}&=150+100=250.
\end{aligned}
\]

Therefore the minimum possible value of \(\sum_{d\mid 250}\max A_d\) is
\[
1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250=499.
\]

\[
\boxed{499}
\]

### 1) Immediate consequences of the condition
Given \(|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)\) for all \(i,j\):

- Taking \(i=j\):  
  \[
  |A_i|=|A_i\cap A_i|=\gcd(i,i)=i.
  \]
- If \(d\mid n\), then \(\gcd(d,n)=d\), so
  \[
  |A_d\cap A_n|=d=|A_d|\implies A_d\subseteq A_n.
  \]
- Since \(\gcd(i,j)\mid i\) and \(\gcd(i,j)\mid j\), we have \(A_{\gcd(i,j)}\subseteq A_i\cap A_j\). But both sets have the same size \(\gcd(i,j)\), hence
  \[
  A_i\cap A_j = A_{\gcd(i,j)}.
  \]

Let \(m_k=\max A_k\). We want to minimize
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250} m_d = m_1+m_2+m_5+m_{10}+m_{25}+m_{50}+m_{125}+m_{250}.
\]

Also, any finite set of positive integers with maximum \(M\) has size \(\le M\). So \(|S|\le \max S\) for any finite \(S\subset\mathbb Z_{>0}\).

---

### 2) Lower bounds via unions
Compute certain union sizes using \(|X\cup Y|=|X|+|Y|-|X\cap Y|\) and \(A_i\cap A_j=A_{\gcd(i,j)}\).

#### (a) \(A_2\) and \(A_5\)
\[
|A_2\cup A_5| = |A_2|+|A_5|-|A_1| = 2+5-1=6.
\]
But \(\max(A_2\cup A_5)=\max(m_2,m_5)\), hence
\[
\max(m_2,m_5)\ge 6.
\]
With \(m_2\ge 2,\ m_5\ge 5\), the minimum possible \(m_2+m_5\) under \(\max(m_2,m_5)\ge 6\) is
\[
m_2+m_5\ge 2+6=8.
\]

#### (b) \(A_{10}\) and \(A_{25}\)
Here \(\gcd(10,25)=5\), so \(A_{10}\cap A_{25}=A_5\) and \(|A_5|=5\). Then
\[
|A_{10}\cup A_{25}| = 10+25-5=30.
\]
Thus \(\max(m_{10},m_{25})\ge 30\). With \(m_{10}\ge 10,\ m_{25}\ge 25\), the smallest sum occurs at \((m_{10},m_{25})=(10,30)\), giving
\[
m_{10}+m_{25}\ge 40.
\]

#### (c) \(A_{50}\) and \(A_{125}\)
\(\gcd(50,125)=25\), so \(A_{50}\cap A_{125}=A_{25}\) and \(|A_{25}|=25\). Then
\[
|A_{50}\cup A_{125}| = 50+125-25=150.
\]
So \(\max(m_{50},m_{125})\ge 150\). With \(m_{50}\ge 50,\ m_{125}\ge 125\), the smallest sum is \((m_{50},m_{125})=(50,150)\), giving
\[
m_{50}+m_{125}\ge 200.
\]

#### (d) \(m_1\) and \(m_{250}\)
\(|A_1|=1\Rightarrow m_1\ge 1\). Also \(|A_{250}|=250\Rightarrow m_{250}\ge 250\).

---

### 3) Combine the bounds
Add the best-possible lower bounds:
\[
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{d\mid 250} m_d
&= (m_1+m_{250}) + (m_2+m_5) + (m_{10}+m_{25}) + (m_{50}+m_{125})\\
&\ge (1+250) + 8 + 40 + 200\\
&= 499.
\end{aligned}
\]
So the desired minimum is at least \(499\).

### 4) Construction achieving 499
A standard (and valid) way to realize the gcd-intersection property is: create disjoint “blocks” \(B_n\) of size \(\varphi(n)\), and define
\[
A_k=\bigcup_{n\mid k} B_n.
\]
Then \(|A_i\cap A_j|=\sum_{n\mid \gcd(i,j)}\varphi(n)=\gcd(i,j)\), and \(|A_k|=k\).

For the divisors of \(250\), assign actual integer labels to the blocks dividing \(250\) as follows (disjoint blocks with the required sizes \(\varphi(n)\)):

- \(B_1=\{1\}\) \(\ (\varphi(1)=1)\)
- \(B_2=\{2\}\) \(\ (\varphi(2)=1)\)
- \(B_5=\{3,4,5,6\}\) \(\ (\varphi(5)=4)\)
- \(B_{10}=\{7,8,9,10\}\) \(\ (\varphi(10)=4)\)
- \(B_{25}=\{11,12,\dots,30\}\) \(\ (\varphi(25)=20)\)
- \(B_{50}=\{31,32,\dots,50\}\) \(\ (\varphi(50)=20)\)
- \(B_{125}=\{51,52,\dots,150\}\) \(\ (\varphi(125)=100)\)
- \(B_{250}=\{151,152,\dots,250\}\) \(\ (\varphi(250)=100)\)

(For \(n\nmid 250\), choose \(B_n\) using integers \(>250\) so they don’t affect any \(\max A_d\) with \(d\mid 250\).)

Then the relevant maxima are:
\[
\begin{aligned}
\max A_1&=1,\\
\max A_2&=2,\\
\max A_5&=6,\\
\max A_{10}&=10,\\
\max A_{25}&=30,\\
\max A_{50}&=50,\\
\max A_{125}&=150,\\
\max A_{250}&=250,
\end{aligned}
\]
so the sum is \(1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250=499\), meeting the lower bound.

Therefore the minimum possible value is \(\boxed{499}\).

### 1) Structural consequences of the condition

Given \(|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)\) for all \(i,j\), note that \(\gcd(i,j)\mid i\) and \(\gcd(i,j)\mid j\), so
\[
A_{\gcd(i,j)}\subseteq A_i,\quad A_{\gcd(i,j)}\subseteq A_j
\]
(because if \(m\mid n\) then \(|A_m\cap A_n|=\gcd(m,n)=m=|A_m|\), hence \(A_m\subseteq A_n\)). Therefore
\[
A_{\gcd(i,j)}\subseteq A_i\cap A_j.
\]
But both sets have the same size \(\gcd(i,j)\), so actually
\[
A_i\cap A_j = A_{\gcd(i,j)}. \tag{1}
\]

Now take any element \(x\) and define \(m(x)\) to be the smallest index with \(x\in A_{m(x)}\). If \(x\in A_n\), then by (1),
\[
x\in A_{m(x)}\cap A_n = A_{\gcd(m(x),n)}.
\]
By minimality of \(m(x)\), we must have \(\gcd(m(x),n)=m(x)\), i.e. \(m(x)\mid n\). Conversely, if \(m(x)\mid n\), then \(A_{m(x)}\subseteq A_n\), so \(x\in A_n\). Hence:

- Each element belongs to \(A_n\) **iff** its minimal index \(m\) divides \(n\).

Thus the elements partition into disjoint blocks \(S_m:=\{x: m(x)=m\}\), and
\[
A_n=\bigcup_{m\mid n} S_m \quad\text{(disjoint union)}. \tag{2}
\]
Taking sizes and using \(|A_n|=|A_n\cap A_n|=\gcd(n,n)=n\),
\[
n = |A_n| = \sum_{m\mid n} |S_m|. \tag{3}
\]
By Möbius inversion, (3) forces \(|S_m|=\varphi(m)\) (Euler totient). So this decomposition (up to relabeling of the actual integers) is forced.

---

### 2) Lower bound on \(\sum_{d\mid 250} \max A_d\)

Divisors of \(250=2\cdot 5^3\) are
\[
1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250.
\]
Let \(M_d:=\max A_d\).

Key fact: If two finite sets \(X,Y\subset\mathbb Z_{>0}\) have maxima \(M_X,M_Y\), then \(X\cup Y\subseteq \{1,2,\dots,\max(M_X,M_Y)\}\), so
\[
|X\cup Y|\le \max(M_X,M_Y). \tag{4}
\]

Also, by (1), \(A_a\cap A_b=A_{\gcd(a,b)}\), so
\[
|A_a\cup A_b| = |A_a|+|A_b|-|A_{\gcd(a,b)}|=a+b-\gcd(a,b). \tag{5}
\]

Now apply (4)+(5) to three pairs:

**(i) \(A_2\) and \(A_5\):**  
\(|A_2\cup A_5|=2+5-1=6\). Hence \(\max(M_2,M_5)\ge 6\). With \(M_2\ge 2\), \(M_5\ge 5\), the minimum possible \(M_2+M_5\) under \(\max(M_2,M_5)\ge 6\) is \(2+6=8\). So
\[
M_2+M_5\ge 8. \tag{6}
\]

**(ii) \(A_{10}\) and \(A_{25}\):**  
\(|A_{10}\cup A_{25}|=10+25-5=30\). Thus \(\max(M_{10},M_{25})\ge 30\). With \(M_{10}\ge 10\), \(M_{25}\ge 25\), the minimum \(M_{10}+M_{25}\) is \(10+30=40\). So
\[
M_{10}+M_{25}\ge 40. \tag{7}
\]

**(iii) \(A_{50}\) and \(A_{125}\):**  
\(|A_{50}\cup A_{125}|=50+125-25=150\). Thus \(\max(M_{50},M_{125})\ge 150\). With \(M_{50}\ge 50\), \(M_{125}\ge 125\), the minimum \(M_{50}+M_{125}\) is \(50+150=200\). So
\[
M_{50}+M_{125}\ge 200. \tag{8}
\]

Finally, \(M_1\ge 1\) and since \(|A_{250}|=250\), we must have \(M_{250}\ge 250\). Hence
\[
M_1+M_{250}\ge 1+250=251. \tag{9}
\]

Summing (6),(7),(8),(9) gives a global lower bound on the required sum:
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250} M_d
\;=\;
(M_1+M_{250})+(M_2+M_5)+(M_{10}+M_{25})+(M_{50}+M_{125})
\;\ge\;
251+8+40+200
=499. \tag{10}
\]

So the minimum is at least \(499\).

---

### 3) Construction achieving the bound \(499\)

For \(d\mid 250\), we must have \(|S_d|=\varphi(d)\). Compute:
\[
\varphi(1)=1,\ \varphi(2)=1,\ \varphi(5)=4,\ \varphi(10)=4,\ \varphi(25)=20,\ \varphi(50)=20,\ \varphi(125)=100,\ \varphi(250)=100.
\]
These sum to \(250\). Assign disjoint blocks of integers as:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&S_1=\{1\},\quad S_2=\{2\},\quad S_5=\{3,4,5,6\},\quad S_{10}=\{7,8,9,10\},\\
&S_{25}=\{11,12,\dots,30\},\quad S_{50}=\{31,32,\dots,50\},\\
&S_{125}=\{51,52,\dots,150\},\quad S_{250}=\{151,152,\dots,250\}.
\end{aligned}
\]
Then define for every \(n\) (in particular for all divisors of \(250\)):
\[
A_n=\bigcup_{d\mid n} S_d.
\]
This guarantees \(|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)\) because
\[
|A_i\cap A_j| = \sum_{d\mid \gcd(i,j)} |S_d|
= \sum_{d\mid \gcd(i,j)} \varphi(d)
= \gcd(i,j).
\]

Now compute maxima for \(d\mid 250\):
\[
\max A_1=1,\ \max A_2=2,\ \max A_5=6,\ \max A_{10}=10,\ \max A_{25}=30,\ \max A_{50}=50,\ \max A_{125}=150,\ \max A_{250}=250.
\]
Sum:
\[
1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250 = 499.
\]
This meets the lower bound, so it is minimal.

### 1) Basic consequences of the condition

Given \(|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)\) for all \(i,j\):

- Taking \(i=j\) gives \(|A_i|=i\).
- If \(i\mid j\), then \(|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)=i=|A_i|\), so  
  \[
  A_i\subseteq A_j.
  \]
- Hence for all \(i,j\), since \(A_{\gcd(i,j)}\subseteq A_i\cap A_j\) and both have the same size \(\gcd(i,j)\), we get the stronger fact
  \[
  A_i\cap A_j = A_{\gcd(i,j)}.
  \]

In particular, for every divisor \(d\mid 250\), we have \(A_d\subseteq A_{250}\) and \(|A_{250}|=250\), so
\[
\max A_{250}\ge 250,
\]
and we can achieve equality by labeling \(A_{250}=\{1,2,\dots,250\}\).

So the problem is to minimize the other maxima under the forced intersection structure.

---

### 2) The forced “new element” decomposition (sizes are Euler \(\varphi\))

Define for each positive integer \(n\) the “new part”
\[
B_n \;=\; A_n\setminus \bigcup_{\substack{d\mid n\\ d<n}} A_d.
\]
Then the \(B_d\) for \(d\mid 250\) are pairwise disjoint, and for every divisor \(n\mid 250\),
\[
A_n = \bigcup_{d\mid n} B_d,
\qquad\text{so}\qquad
|A_n| = \sum_{d\mid n} |B_d|.
\]
Since \(|A_n|=n\), Möbius inversion forces
\[
|B_n|=\varphi(n).
\]

For \(250=2\cdot 5^3\), its divisors are
\[
1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250,
\]
and the corresponding \(\varphi\)-sizes are:
\[
\varphi(1)=1,\ \varphi(2)=1,\ \varphi(5)=4,\ \varphi(10)=4,\ \varphi(25)=20,\ \varphi(50)=20,\ \varphi(125)=100,\ \varphi(250)=100.
\]
These sum to \(250\), i.e. exactly \(|A_{250}|\).

Thus inside \(A_{250}\), we have 8 disjoint blocks \(B_d\) of these sizes, and each \(A_n\) is the union of blocks \(B_d\) with \(d\mid n\).

---

### 3) Labeling as an ordering (scheduling viewpoint)

Once \(A_{250}=\{1,2,\dots,250\}\) is fixed, assigning labels is equivalent to choosing an order in which the blocks \(B_d\) occupy initial segments of \(\{1,\dots,250\}\).

For any divisor \(n\mid 250\), \(\max A_n\) is the largest label appearing among blocks \(B_d\) with \(d\mid n\), i.e. the time when the last required block finishes.

To minimize the sum of these “finish times,” we should:
- Put \(B_{250}\) (used only by \(A_{250}\)) **last**.
- Put large blocks that are needed by few sets late, and complete smaller sets as early as possible.

A clean exchange argument forces the optimal order of the blocks (excluding \(B_{250}\)):

1. \(B_1\) must be first (size 1, and \(A_1\) completes only then).
2. \(B_2\) should come before \(B_5\): swapping \(B_2\) earlier reduces \(\max A_2\) by 4 while not helping any other set enough to compensate.
3. After \(B_5\), do \(B_{10}\) before \(B_{25}\): \(B_{10}\) (size 4) completes \(A_{10}\) and reduces the number of unfinished sets before the long \(B_{25}\) (size 20).
4. \(B_{25}\) must precede \(B_{50}\) and \(B_{125}\) (otherwise neither \(A_{50}\) nor \(A_{125}\) can finish, so putting \(B_{50}\) or \(B_{125}\) earlier only delays).
5. After \(B_{25}\), do \(B_{50}\) (20) before \(B_{125}\) (100) since both remaining sets \(A_{50},A_{125}\) are unfinished; doing the shorter one first reduces the time with two unfinished sets.

Therefore the optimal block order is:
\[
B_1,\ B_2,\ B_5,\ B_{10},\ B_{25},\ B_{50},\ B_{125},\ B_{250}.
\]

---

### 4) Compute the resulting maxima

Using cumulative sums of block sizes:

- After \(B_1\): \(1\)
- After \(B_2\): \(1+1=2\)
- After \(B_5\): \(2+4=6\)
- After \(B_{10}\): \(6+4=10\)
- After \(B_{25}\): \(10+20=30\)
- After \(B_{50}\): \(30+20=50\)
- After \(B_{125}\): \(50+100=150\)
- After \(B_{250}\): \(150+100=250\)

Now each \(A_d\) is the union of \(B_e\) for \(e\mid d\), so \(\max A_d\) is the completion time of the latest such block:

\[
\begin{aligned}
\max A_1 &= 1,\\
\max A_2 &= 2,\\
\max A_5 &= 6,\\
\max A_{10} &= 10,\\
\max A_{25} &= 30,\\
\max A_{50} &= 50,\\
\max A_{125} &= 150,\\
\max A_{250} &= 250.
\end{aligned}
\]

Sum:
\[
1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250 = 499.
\]

This is achievable, e.g. by labeling consecutively:
\[
\begin{aligned}
B_1&=\{1\},\ B_2=\{2\},\ B_5=\{3,4,5,6\},\ B_{10}=\{7,8,9,10\},\\
B_{25}&=\{11,\dots,30\},\ B_{50}=\{31,\dots,50\},\ B_{125}=\{51,\dots,150\},\ B_{250}=\{151,\dots,250\},
\end{aligned}
\]
and defining \(A_d=\bigcup_{e\mid d}B_e\). Then \(|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)\) holds.

Because \(|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)\), in particular \(|A_n|=|A_n\cap A_n|=n\).

### 1) Forced containment for divisibility
If \(a\mid b\), then \(|A_a\cap A_b|=\gcd(a,b)=a=|A_a|\), hence
\[
A_a\subseteq A_b.
\]
So among the divisors of \(250\), the sets satisfy all divisibility containments, e.g. \(A_1\subseteq A_2\subseteq A_{10}\subseteq A_{50}\subseteq A_{250}\), etc.

### 2) The canonical “new elements at level \(d\)” decomposition
Restrict attention to indices \(d\mid 250\). Define for each such \(d\),
\[
D_d \;=\; A_d\setminus \bigcup_{\substack{e\mid d\\ e<d}} A_e.
\]
Then the \(D_d\) are pairwise disjoint and
\[
A_d=\bigcup_{e\mid d} D_e \quad\text{(disjoint union)}.
\]
Taking cardinalities gives, for each \(d\mid 250\),
\[
d=|A_d|=\sum_{e\mid d} |D_e|.
\]
By Möbius inversion on divisors, this forces
\[
|D_d|=\varphi(d).
\]
For \(250=2\cdot 5^3\), the divisors are
\[
1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250
\]
and the sizes are
\[
\varphi(1)=1,\;\varphi(2)=1,\;\varphi(5)=4,\;\varphi(10)=4,\;\varphi(25)=20,\;\varphi(50)=20,\;\varphi(125)=100,\;\varphi(250)=100,
\]
summing to \(250\). Hence \(A_{250}\) is partitioned into these 8 blocks \(D_d\).

For any divisor \(m\mid 250\),
\[
\max A_m = \max_{e\mid m} \big(\max D_e\big).
\]
So the problem becomes: assign distinct positive integer labels to the \(250\) elements (equivalently to the blocks \(D_d\)) to minimize \(\sum_{m\mid 250}\max A_m\).

### 3) We may assume \(A_{250}=\{1,2,\dots,250\}\) and blocks get initial segments
Since \(A_{250}\) itself appears in the sum, \(\max A_{250}\) is minimized by using the labels \(1,2,\dots,250\), giving \(\max A_{250}=250\).

Given an order in which we “allocate” labels to blocks, the best way to minimize each block maximum is to give each block the smallest available labels; thus each block occupies a consecutive segment, and the maximum label in that block is the cumulative total of block sizes up to that point.

So we need only choose an order of the 8 blocks; then the block maxima are the prefix sums of their \(\varphi\)-sizes.

### 4) Optimal order must respect divisibility
If \(e\mid d\) but block \(D_d\) is placed before \(D_e\), swapping them cannot increase any \(\max A_m\): every \(A_m\) that needs \(D_d\) also needs \(D_e\), so completing \(D_e\) earlier can only help sets that require \(e\) but not \(d\), and does not delay any set that requires \(d\). Hence there is an optimal order with all divisibility prerequisites first.

Thus we need a topological order of the divisor-poset. The forced relative orders (besides ties) are:
\[
1 \prec 2,\; 1\prec 5,\; 2,5\prec 10,\; 5\prec 25,\; 10,25\prec 50,\; 25\prec 125,\; 50,125\prec 250.
\]

Now compare remaining choices:

- Between \(2\) (size \(1\)) and \(5\) (size \(4\)) right after \(1\): swapping only affects \(\max A_2\) and \(\max A_5\), and putting the smaller block first gives smaller sum. So \(2\) before \(5\).
- After \(1,2,5\), both \(10\) (size \(4\)) and \(25\) (size \(20\)) are available. Swapping them only affects \(\max A_{10}\) and \(\max A_{25}\) (later sets depend on having both anyway), and clearly \(10\) before \(25\) is better.
- After \(10,25\), both \(50\) (size \(20\)) and \(125\) (size \(100\)) are available. Swapping them only affects \(\max A_{50}\) and \(\max A_{125}\), and \(50\) before \(125\) is better.

Therefore the optimal block order is
\[
1,\;2,\;5,\;10,\;25,\;50,\;125,\;250.
\]

### 5) Compute the resulting maxima and the sum
Prefix sums of sizes \(1,1,4,4,20,20,100,100\) are:
\[
1,\;2,\;6,\;10,\;30,\;50,\;150,\;250.
\]
Thus
\[
\max A_1=1,\;
\max A_2=2,\;
\max A_5=6,\;
\max A_{10}=10,\;
\max A_{25}=30,\;
\max A_{50}=50,\;
\max A_{125}=150,\;
\max A_{250}=250.
\]
Sum:
\[
1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250=499.
\]

This is achievable by labeling the blocks in that order with consecutive integers, and the general construction \(A_n=\bigcup_{d\mid n} D_d\) (with \(|D_d|=\varphi(d)\)) extends to all positive integers, so the global condition is satisfied.

\[
\boxed{499}
\]

Let \(m_d=\max A_d\) for each \(d\mid 250\).

### 1) Sizes and a useful inequality
Taking \(i=j\) in \(|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)\) gives
\[
|A_i|=|A_i\cap A_i|=\gcd(i,i)=i.
\]
So \(|A_d|=d\) for every \(d\).

For any finite set \(S\) of positive integers, \(\max S\ge |S|\) (since \(S\subseteq\{1,2,\dots,\max S\}\)). Hence for any \(i,j\),
\[
\max(m_i,m_j)=\max(A_i\cup A_j)\ge |A_i\cup A_j|.
\]
But
\[
|A_i\cup A_j|=|A_i|+|A_j|-|A_i\cap A_j|
=i+j-\gcd(i,j).
\]
Therefore
\[
\boxed{\max(m_i,m_j)\ge i+j-\gcd(i,j)} \qquad (\ast)
\]

### 2) Lower bound for the desired sum
The divisors of \(250\) are
\[
1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250,
\]
and always \(m_d\ge d\). The “baseline” sum is
\[
1+2+5+10+25+50+125+250=468.
\]

Now apply \((\ast)\) to three key pairs:

- For \((2,5)\): \(\gcd(2,5)=1\), so \(\max(m_2,m_5)\ge 2+5-1=6\).
  With \(m_2\ge 2\) and \(m_5\ge 5\), the minimum possible \(m_2+m_5\) under \(\max(m_2,m_5)\ge 6\) is \(2+6=8\) (better than \(6+5=11\)).

- For \((10,25)\): \(\gcd(10,25)=5\), so \(\max(m_{10},m_{25})\ge 10+25-5=30\).
  Minimizing \(m_{10}+m_{25}\) with \(m_{10}\ge 10,m_{25}\ge 25\) forces \(m_{10}=10,m_{25}=30\), giving \(m_{10}+m_{25}\ge 40\).

- For \((50,125)\): \(\gcd(50,125)=25\), so \(\max(m_{50},m_{125})\ge 50+125-25=150\).
  Minimizing with \(m_{50}\ge 50,m_{125}\ge 125\) forces \(m_{50}=50,m_{125}=150\), so \(m_{50}+m_{125}\ge 200\).

Thus
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250} m_d
= m_1+m_{250}+(m_2+m_5)+(m_{10}+m_{25})+(m_{50}+m_{125})
\ge 1+250+8+40+200=499.
\]
So the sum is at least \(499\).

### 3) Construction achieving \(499\)
Create disjoint blocks \(B_n\) of sizes \(\varphi(n)\) (Euler totient), and define
\[
A_n=\bigcup_{d\mid n} B_d.
\]
Then for any \(i,j\),
\[
A_i\cap A_j=\bigcup_{d\mid \gcd(i,j)} B_d
\]
(disjoint union), so
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\sum_{d\mid \gcd(i,j)} \varphi(d)=\gcd(i,j),
\]
as required.

For the divisors of \(250\), take:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&B_1=\{1\} \quad (\varphi(1)=1),\\
&B_2=\{2\} \quad (\varphi(2)=1),\\
&B_5=\{3,4,5,6\} \quad (\varphi(5)=4),\\
&B_{10}=\{7,8,9,10\} \quad (\varphi(10)=4),\\
&B_{25}=\{11,12,\dots,30\} \quad (\varphi(25)=20),\\
&B_{50}=\{31,32,\dots,50\} \quad (\varphi(50)=20),\\
&B_{125}=\{51,52,\dots,150\} \quad (\varphi(125)=100),\\
&B_{250}=\{151,152,\dots,250\} \quad (\varphi(250)=100).
\end{aligned}
\]
Then
\[
\max A_1=1,\ \max A_2=2,\ \max A_5=6,\ \max A_{10}=10,\ \max A_{25}=30,\ \max A_{50}=50,\ \max A_{125}=150,\ \max A_{250}=250,
\]
so
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250} \max A_d=1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250=499.
\]

This matches the lower bound, so it is minimal.

\[
\boxed{499}
\]

From \(|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)\), taking \(i=j\) gives
\[
|A_i|=\gcd(i,i)=i.
\]
Also, if \(i\mid j\), then \(|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)=i=|A_i|\), hence
\[
A_i\subseteq A_j\qquad (i\mid j).
\]

The divisors of \(250=2\cdot 5^3\) are
\[
1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250.
\]
So we have the inclusions \(A_1\subset A_2\subset A_{10}\subset A_{50}\subset A_{250}\), and \(A_1\subset A_5\subset A_{25}\subset A_{125}\subset A_{250}\), etc.

---

## 1) Forced disjoint “new parts” and their sizes
Define the “new part” added at each divisor \(d\mid 250\) by subtracting what must already be present from smaller divisors:

- \(B_1:=A_1\), so \(|B_1|=1\).
- Since \(A_1\subset A_2\), set \(B_2:=A_2\setminus A_1\), so \(|B_2|=2-1=1\).
- Since \(A_1\subset A_5\), set \(B_5:=A_5\setminus A_1\), so \(|B_5|=5-1=4\).

Now \(A_2\cap A_5\) has size \(\gcd(2,5)=1\), and \(A_1\subseteq A_2\cap A_5\), so
\[
A_2\cap A_5=A_1.
\]
Also \(A_2\subset A_{10}\) and \(A_5\subset A_{10}\). Thus \(A_2\cup A_5\subset A_{10}\), and
\[
|A_2\cup A_5|=|A_2|+|A_5|-|A_2\cap A_5|=2+5-1=6.
\]
So define
\[
B_{10}:=A_{10}\setminus (A_2\cup A_5),\quad |B_{10}|=10-6=4.
\]

Similarly, \(A_5\subset A_{25}\), so
\[
B_{25}:=A_{25}\setminus A_5,\quad |B_{25}|=25-5=20.
\]
Next \(|A_{10}\cap A_{25}|=\gcd(10,25)=5\), and \(A_5\subseteq A_{10}\cap A_{25}\), so \(A_{10}\cap A_{25}=A_5\). Hence
\[
|A_{10}\cup A_{25}|=10+25-5=30,
\]
and since \(A_{10}\cup A_{25}\subset A_{50}\),
\[
B_{50}:=A_{50}\setminus (A_{10}\cup A_{25}),\quad |B_{50}|=50-30=20.
\]

Next \(|A_{25}\cap A_{10}|=5\) and \(|A_{25}\cap A_{125}|=\gcd(25,125)=25\) gives \(A_{25}\subset A_{125}\), so define
\[
B_{125}:=A_{125}\setminus A_{25},\quad |B_{125}|=125-25=100.
\]

Finally, \(\gcd(50,125)=25\) implies \(A_{50}\cap A_{125}=A_{25}\). Thus
\[
|A_{50}\cup A_{125}|=50+125-25=150,
\]
and since \(A_{50}\cup A_{125}\subset A_{250}\),
\[
B_{250}:=A_{250}\setminus (A_{50}\cup A_{125}),\quad |B_{250}|=250-150=100.
\]

All these \(B_d\) are pairwise disjoint by construction, and each \(A_d\) is exactly the union of the \(B_e\) for \(e\mid d\). So the decomposition is forced (up to relabeling of elements):
\[
|B_1|=1,\ |B_2|=1,\ |B_5|=4,\ |B_{10}|=4,\ |B_{25}|=20,\ |B_{50}|=20,\ |B_{125}|=100,\ |B_{250}|=100.
\]

---

## 2) Turning the minimization into an ordering problem
Since \(|A_{250}|=250\), to minimize \(\max A_{250}\) we must take \(A_{250}=\{1,2,\dots,250\}\), so \(\max A_{250}=250\) is fixed.

All other \(A_d\) (\(d\mid 250\)) are subsets of \(A_{250}\), and their elements are exactly unions of the disjoint blocks \(B_e\). If we assign the labels \(1,2,\dots,250\) to the elements block-by-block in some order, each \(\max A_d\) becomes the endpoint of the last block among those \(B_e\) with \(e\mid d\).

Because only maxima matter, in an optimum we never benefit from interleaving two blocks: splitting a block can only push its maximum label larger without helping any other maximum. Thus we may assume each block \(B_e\) occupies a consecutive interval of labels, and the problem is to choose the best order of the blocks.

Also, \(B_{250}\) appears only in \(A_{250}\) among our summed sets, so placing \(B_{250}\) earlier would only increase other maxima while leaving \(\max A_{250}=250\). Hence \(B_{250}\) should be last.

Now order the remaining blocks:
- \(B_1\) must come first (it is contained in every \(A_d\), so anything before it delays all maxima).
- After \(B_1\), placing \(B_2\) next is optimal because it is the only block whose completion can finish \(A_2\); any other block placed before \(B_2\) delays \(\max A_2\) by at least \(4\) (or \(20,100\)) while not allowing any other \(A_d\) to finish sooner.
- After \(B_1,B_2\), the same logic forces \(B_5\) next to finish \(A_5\).
- After \(B_1,B_2,B_5\), block \(B_{10}\) (size \(4\)) finishes \(A_{10}\) and should precede \(B_{25}\) (size \(20\)).
- After that, all remaining unsatisfied sets require \(B_{25}\), so \(B_{25}\) must come next.
- Finally, between \(B_{50}\) (size \(20\)) and \(B_{125}\) (size \(100\)), doing \(B_{50}\) first makes \(\max A_{50}\) smaller and cannot increase \(\max A_{125}\) (which is governed by the later \(B_{125}\)), so \(B_{50}\) before \(B_{125}\).

Thus the optimal order is
\[
B_1,\ B_2,\ B_5,\ B_{10},\ B_{25},\ B_{50},\ B_{125},\ B_{250}.
\]

---

## 3) Compute the resulting maxima
Cumulative endpoints (i.e. maximum labels within each prefix):

- after \(B_1\): \(1\)
- after \(B_2\): \(2\)
- after \(B_5\): \(6\)
- after \(B_{10}\): \(10\)
- after \(B_{25}\): \(30\)
- after \(B_{50}\): \(50\)
- after \(B_{125}\): \(150\)
- after \(B_{250}\): \(250\)

Now each \(A_d\) is the union of \(B_e\) with \(e\mid d\), so its maximum is the endpoint of the last such block:

\[
\begin{aligned}
\max A_1&=1,\\
\max A_2&=2,\\
\max A_5&=6,\\
\max A_{10}&=10,\\
\max A_{25}&=30,\\
\max A_{50}&=50,\\
\max A_{125}&=150,\\
\max A_{250}&=250.
\end{aligned}
\]
Therefore
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250}\max A_d
=1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250
=499.
\]

\[
\boxed{499}
\]

### 1) Immediate structural consequences

Given \(|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)\) for all \(i,j\):

- Taking \(i=j\) gives \(|A_i|=i\).
- If \(i\mid j\), then \(\gcd(i,j)=i\), so
  \[
  |A_i\cap A_j|=i=|A_i|\implies A_i\subseteq A_j.
  \]
- Let \(g=\gcd(i,j)\). Since \(g\mid i\) and \(g\mid j\), we have \(A_g\subseteq A_i\cap A_j\), and
  \[
  |A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)=g=|A_g|\implies A_i\cap A_j=A_g.
  \]

So the family behaves exactly like the gcd-meet structure.

---

### 2) “Minimal index” decomposition and forced block sizes

For an element \(x\), define its **minimal index**
\[
m(x)=\min\{n: x\in A_n\}.
\]
Because \(A_a\subseteq A_b\) iff \(a\mid b\), one checks:
\[
x\in A_n \iff m(x)\mid n.
\]
Let
\[
B_d=\{x: m(x)=d\},\qquad b_d=|B_d|.
\]
Then
\[
|A_n|=\sum_{d\mid n} b_d = n.
\]
By Möbius inversion, the unique solution is
\[
b_d=\varphi(d).
\]

In particular, for divisors of \(250=2\cdot 5^3\), the divisors are
\[
1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250,
\]
and their forced block sizes are:
\[
\varphi(1)=1,\ \varphi(2)=1,\ \varphi(5)=4,\ \varphi(10)=4,\ \varphi(25)=20,\ \varphi(50)=20,\ \varphi(125)=100,\ \varphi(250)=100,
\]
summing to \(250\).

Also, for each divisor \(d\mid 250\),
\[
A_d=\bigcup_{e\mid d} B_e.
\]

---

### 3) Reformulating the objective in terms of block maxima

Let
\[
M_e=\max(B_e).
\]
Then
\[
\max(A_d)=\max_{e\mid d} M_e.
\]

**Key monotonicity.** If \(e\mid f\), then every set \(A_d\) that contains \(B_f\) also contains \(B_e\). If \(M_e>M_f\), then \(B_f\) never determines any \(\max(A_d)\) where it appears; swapping large labels from \(B_e\) into \(B_f\) can only decrease (never increase) all relevant maxima. Thus we may assume in an optimal arrangement that
\[
e\mid f\implies M_e\le M_f.
\]
Under this monotonicity, the largest divisor \(d\) dominates within its own divisor set, hence
\[
\max(A_d)=M_d\quad\text{for }d\mid 250.
\]
So we want to minimize
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250} M_d.
\]

---

### 4) Scheduling viewpoint: contiguous labeling is optimal under monotonicity

Since the \(250\) elements in \(A_{250}\) must be distinct positive integers, \(\max(A_{250})\ge 250\), and we may (and should) use labels \(1,2,\dots,250\).

With monotonicity \(M_e\le M_f\) whenever \(e\mid f\), the optimal way to realize given maxima is to assign the blocks contiguously in increasing order of their maxima (any “gaps” waste small labels and cannot improve any maximum). Thus we are effectively scheduling 8 “jobs” with processing times \(\varphi(d)\) and precedence constraints \(e\mid f \Rightarrow e\) before \(f\), minimizing the sum of completion times. The completion time of job \(d\) equals \(M_d\).

The precedence constraints among our divisors force:
- \(1\) first.
- After \(1\), both \(2\) and \(5\) are available; since \(1<4\), \(2\) should be before \(5\) (adjacent-swap argument).
- After \(2,5\), both \(10\) and \(25\) are available; since \(4<20\), schedule \(10\) before \(25\).
- After \(10,25\), both \(50\) and \(125\) are available; since \(20<100\), schedule \(50\) before \(125\).
- Finally \(250\).

Thus the unique optimal order is:
\[
1,\ 2,\ 5,\ 10,\ 25,\ 50,\ 125,\ 250.
\]

Compute completion times (cumulative sums of \(\varphi\)):

\[
\begin{array}{c|c|c}
d & \varphi(d) & M_d \\
\hline
1&1&1\\
2&1&2\\
5&4&6\\
10&4&10\\
25&20&30\\
50&20&50\\
125&100&150\\
250&100&250
\end{array}
\]

Therefore
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250} \max A_d=\sum_{d\mid 250} M_d
=1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250=499.
\]

This is achievable by taking \(B_1=\{1\}\), \(B_2=\{2\}\), \(B_5=\{3,4,5,6\}\), \(B_{10}=\{7,8,9,10\}\), \(B_{25}=\{11,\dots,30\}\), \(B_{50}=\{31,\dots,50\}\), \(B_{125}=\{51,\dots,150\}\), \(B_{250}=\{151,\dots,250\}\), and extending similarly for other \(n\).

\[
\boxed{499}
\]

From \(i=j\) we get
\[
|A_i|=|A_i\cap A_i|=\gcd(i,i)=i.
\]
If \(i\mid j\), then \(|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)=i=|A_i|\), hence \(A_i\subseteq A_j\).

Therefore, for any \(i,j\),
\[
A_{\gcd(i,j)}\subseteq A_i\cap A_j,\qquad |A_{\gcd(i,j)}|=\gcd(i,j)=|A_i\cap A_j|,
\]
so in fact
\[
A_i\cap A_j = A_{\gcd(i,j)}.
\]

### 1) Decompose \(A_{250}\) into “new elements” blocks
Let \(D\) be the divisors of \(250\):
\[
D=\{1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250\}.
\]
For each \(d\in D\), define the block
\[
B_d \;=\; A_d \setminus \bigcup_{\substack{e\mid d\\ e<d}} A_e.
\]
Because \(A_e\subseteq A_d\) for \(e\mid d\), these blocks are disjoint, and
\[
A_d \;=\;\bigsqcup_{e\mid d} B_e
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
d=|A_d|=\sum_{e\mid d} |B_e|.
\]
By Möbius inversion, the unique solution is \(|B_d|=\varphi(d)\). Hence
\[
\begin{array}{c|cccccccc}
d & 1&2&5&10&25&50&125&250\\ \hline
|B_d|=\varphi(d)&1&1&4&4&20&20&100&100
\end{array}
\]
In particular, \(|A_{250}|=250\), so \(A_{250}\) has 250 distinct positive integers, hence \(\max A_{250}\ge 250\). WLOG (by relabeling the 250 elements in increasing order) we may take
\[
A_{250}=\{1,2,\dots,250\},
\]
so \(\max A_{250}=250\).

For each \(d\in D\),
\[
\max A_d = \max\Big(\bigcup_{e\mid d} B_e\Big)
\]
depends only on how we assign the labels \(1,2,\dots,250\) to the disjoint blocks \(B_e\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2) Scheduling interpretation (and why divisors should come earlier)
Think of each block \(B_d\) as a “job” of processing time \(\varphi(d)\). If we assign consecutive labels to the blocks in some order, then the last label used by \(B_d\) is the completion time \(C_d\), and (because \(A_d\) is the union of blocks \(B_e\) with \(e\mid d\)) we get \(\max A_d = \max_{e\mid d} C_e\).

In an optimal arrangement, if \(e\mid d\), placing \(B_d\) before \(B_e\) is never helpful: every set \(A_d\) that needs \(B_d\) also needs \(B_e\), so delaying \(B_e\) delays \(\max A_d\) anyway, and it can only worsen other maxima. Thus we may assume the blocks are ordered so that
\[
e\mid d \implies B_e \text{ is placed before } B_d.
\]
Under this assumption, for each \(d\), \(B_d\) is the last required block for \(A_d\), hence
\[
\max A_d = C_d.
\]
So our goal becomes: find a topological order of the blocks (respecting divisibility) minimizing \(\sum_{d\in D} C_d\).

The precedence constraints are:
- \(1\) before all;
- \(2\) before \(10,50,250\);
- \(5\) before \(10,25,50,125,250\);
- \(10\) before \(50,250\);
- \(25\) before \(50,125,250\);
- \(50\) and \(125\) before \(250\).

The processing times are:
\[
1,1,4,4,20,20,100,100 \quad\text{for } d=1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250.
\]

A shortest-available-first choice is forced and optimal here:
- Start with \(1\) (forced).
- Available: \(2\) (1) and \(5\) (4), take \(2\).
- Then \(5\) is forced.
- Available: \(10\) (4) and \(25\) (20), take \(10\).
- Then \(25\).
- Available: \(50\) (20) and \(125\) (100), take \(50\).
- Then \(125\).
- Then \(250\).

Thus the optimal order is
\[
1,\ 2,\ 5,\ 10,\ 25,\ 50,\ 125,\ 250.
\]
The completion times (cumulative sums of \(\varphi\)) are:
\[
C_1=1,\ C_2=2,\ C_5=6,\ C_{10}=10,\ C_{25}=30,\ C_{50}=50,\ C_{125}=150,\ C_{250}=250.
\]
Hence the minimum possible sum is
\[
1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250 = 499.
\]

### 3) Explicit construction attaining 499
Assign the blocks as consecutive integers in that order:
\[
\begin{aligned}
&B_1=\{1\},\quad B_2=\{2\},\quad B_5=\{3,4,5,6\},\quad B_{10}=\{7,8,9,10\},\\
&B_{25}=\{11,\dots,30\},\quad B_{50}=\{31,\dots,50\},\quad B_{125}=\{51,\dots,150\},\quad B_{250}=\{151,\dots,250\}.
\end{aligned}
\]
Define \(A_n=\bigcup_{d\mid n} B_d\) for all \(n\) (and for \(d\nmid 250\) choose disjoint blocks using integers \(>250\)). Then \(|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)\) holds, and the maxima for \(d\mid 250\) are exactly the \(C_d\) above, giving sum \(499\).

Therefore the minimum is \(\boxed{499}\).
